coredata.dc:description: The influences of carbon quantum dot nanoparticles in diesel-biodiesel mixture (D80MCB20) were researched on performance, combustion and exhaust emissions. Test engine was run at 2200 rpm including 4.12, 9.61, 15.10 and 20.60 Nm engine loads. ID period was shortened using nanoparticle apart from D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm. Nanoparticle caused to decrease SFC. CO and HC declined by about 0.76%, 18.02%, 20.30% and 19.48%, 28.57% and 31.16% in case of D80MCB20 CQD 60 ppm, D80MCB20 CQD 120 ppm and D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm usage according to D80MCB20 at 20.60 Nm respectively. Nonetheless, 48.29%, 55.44% and 56.26% increases were specified on NOx using D80MCB20 CQD 60 ppm, D80MCB20 CQD 120 ppm and D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm compared to D80MCB20 at 20.60 Nm. It was seen that the carbon quantum dot nanoparticle additive into the diesel-microwave corn biodiesel mixture was a potential candidate to boost performance and reduce CO, HC and soot emissions.

row.entry: Diesel + microwave corn oil biodiesel + CQD
With the increase in additive, SFC, CA50, CD, COVimep, MPRR, RI, CO2 and NOx increased while CO, HC and soot decreased. As the CQD ratio increased, cylinder pressure increased significantly.

With the add in additive; BSFC and BTE increased while CO and NOx decreased.
With the add in additive; BSFC, BTE, CO2 increased while CO, NOx and HC decreased.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The influences of carbon quantum dot nanoparticles in diesel-biodiesel mixture (D80MCB20) were researched on performance, combustion and exhaust emissions. Test engine was run at 2200 rpm including 4.12, 9.61, 15.10 and 20.60 Nm engine loads. ID period was shortened using nanoparticle apart from D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm. Nanoparticle caused to decrease SFC. CO and HC declined by about 0.76%, 18.02%, 20.30% and 19.48%, 28.57% and 31.16% in case of D80MCB20 CQD 60 ppm, D80MCB20 CQD 120 ppm and D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm usage according to D80MCB20 at 20.60 Nm respectively. Nonetheless, 48.29%, 55.44% and 56.26% increases were specified on NOx using D80MCB20 CQD 60 ppm, D80MCB20 CQD 120 ppm and D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm compared to D80MCB20 at 20.60 Nm. It was seen that the carbon quantum dot nanoparticle additive into the diesel-microwave corn biodiesel mixture was a potential candidate to boost performance and reduce CO, HC and soot emissions.

ce:section.ce:para: Reducing emissions and improving performance are key difficulties directing researchers to make investigations in diesel engines because they have been widely used in freight transportation, agriculture and industry [1–6]. Alternative fuels have undertaken critical functions [1–3,7–10]. Biodiesel is utilized for obtaining reasonable performance and declining emissions. However, some difficulties face with like higher density, viscosity and higher NOx in diesel engines. Microwave production is seen as the perfect method to enhance better fuel specifications and obtain developed chemical reactions during combustion [1]. On the other side, nanoparticles are anticipated as potential candidates to improve fuel characterization and combustion stability in diesel-biodiesel fuel blends. Nanofluids and nanoparticles tend to increase heat transfer due to their high surface area [11,12]. In addition, nanofluids can also cause secondary atomization of fuel droplets [13]. Adding nanofluids to the fuel acts as a catalyst. The combination of these effects ensures complete and homogeneous combustion of the nanofuel sent to the cylinder. As a result of complete combustion, the heat energy obtained from the fuel and the pressure in the cylinder increase. This contributes to increased engine performance and reduced emissions such as HC and soot. So, it is worth to mention that the existence of nanoparticle in biodiesel fuel mixtures is expected to provide better pulverization and combustion development and reduce CO, HC and smoke emissions [8–15]. Several discussions have been made in recent publications to see the effects of diesel-biodiesel mixture with nanoparticle addition. Bidir et al. [10] reviewed the influences of nanoparticle on the production of biodiesel and performance and emissions in compression ignition engines. It was mentioned that nanoparticle is used as catalyst in biodiesel production. Nanoparticle presents high thermal conductivity and develops the combustion. Lakshmi et al. [16] emphasized the importance of nanoparticle usage in terms of performance and emissions. They implied that nanoparticle has higher reactivation, flash and cloud points and advanced calorific value. Mass and heat transfer are also critical parameters owing to high thermal conductivity of nanoparticle. Kalaimurugan et al. [17] investigated the influences of copper oxide (CuO2) nanoparticle into neochloris oleoabundans methyl ester-diesel mixture (B20) in a CI engine. Better combustion and reduction on emissions were found using nanoparticle in fuel blend. Adding CuO2 presented higher pressure rise rate, cylinder pressure, where as lower heat release according to B20. Mujtaba et al. [18] made a study to see the influences of nanoparticle and oxygenated alcohols with diesel/biodiesel mixture (B30). DMC and DEE were blended with biodiesel blend and CNT and TiO2 were added into obtained fuel. Test outcomes showed that B30 + TiO2 ternary blend presented lower BSFC by about 4.1% according to test fuels. On the other hand, 9.88% rise was found on BTE while 21.4% and 29.9% decrease on HC and CO using B30 + DMC respectively compared to test fuels. Rangabashiam et al. [19] observed the functions of zinc oxide nanoparticle added into cashew nut shell biodiesel on emissions. Adding 10 nm nanoparticle resulted in declining HC, CO, NOx and smoke emissions. In work [20], some properties of diesel-biodiesel mixture including CeO2 nanoparticles were reviewed and the changes on atomization and microexplosion were handled. Analysis has been performed related to influences of CeO2 nanoparticles on combustion process, performance and emissions. It was deduced that CeO2 nanoparticles showed favorable influence on toxic emissions and increase power and thermal efficiency in general. Tosun and Özcanlı [21] tested the aluminum oxide (Al2O3) nanoparticle influence with diesel-soybean biodiesel-hydrogen mixtures. It was seen that nanoparticle and hydrogen supply caused smoother performance. However, NOx raised for all test fuels according to diesel. H–B20-A presented higher power by 6.64% and decrease on BSFC by 2.72% according to diesel. Rangabashiam et al. [22] examined the influences of TiO2 nanoparticle addition (50, 100 ppm) into Pongamia biodiesel (PBD100) on combustion, performance and emissions. 100 ppm nanoparticle addition provided a decrease on smoke and HC emissions by about 2.7% and 2.1% respectively according to PBD100 at full load. Ağbulut et al. [23] discussed the influences of metal-oxide based nanoparticles and biodiesel mixtures on performance, combustion, emission, noise and vibration properties. HC, CO and NOx declined using biodiesel and nanoparticle mixtures according to D100. Kalaimurugan et al. [24] discussed the combustion using neochloris oleoabundans algae biodiesel with CuO2 nanoparticle addition. They demonstrated that biodiesel mixtures with CuO2 exhibited better combustion compared to B20. Mehregan and Moghiman [25] intended to see the influences of cobalt oxide and manganese oxide nanoparticles in B20 (80% diesel and 20% waste frying oil biodiesel) on NOx emissions. Nanoparticles reduced NOx while urea-SCR system was more efficient on reducing NOx than nanoparticles. Ce–ZnO nanoparticle was experimented while mixing with a soybean biodiesel mixture on combustion, performance and emissions. It was determined that NOx raised using 50 ppm Ce–ZnO nanoparticle in SBME25 whereas 18.1% and 20.66% increase on HRR and BTE compared to SBME25 [26]. Kumar et al. [27] investigated the effects of alumina nanoparticle added into B25 (25% rubber seed biodiesel mixture) in a diesel engine. BTE increased by about 4.8% using B25 with 50% alumina nanoparticles compared to B25. Jaikumar et al. [28] mixed with B20 (flaxseed oil biodiesel mixture) with Cr2O3 nanoparticle additive with the existence of dispersant (QPAN 80) and experimented in a compression ignition engine including various loads. It was reported that cylinder pressure and net heat release rate were improved by 11.61% and 18.66% respectively, for B20 with 75 ppm Cr2O3 and 1:1 dispersant according to diesel at maximum load. Opuz et al. [29] observed the influences of metallic fuel additive with canola oil biodiesel mixture (D80TCB20). SFC declined by 17.69%, 8.70% and 3.08% with D80TCB20C150, D80TCB20C100 and D80TCB20C50 according to D80TCB20 at 15.10 Nm. Jaikumar et al. [30] mentioned that cobalt chloride (CoCl2) and copper chloride (CuCl2) nanoparticle addition into diesel-linseed oil biodiesel mixture (B20) caused to enhance BTE. Bitire et al. [31] reviewed the influences of copper oxide addition on combustion and performance and implied the potential to decline NOx. In work [32], the addition of graphene oxide into three type biodiesel feedstock-diesel mixtures produced lower CO, UHC and BSFC while higher NOx. Srinivasan et al. [33] researched the influences of alumina and titanium oxide nanoparticle addition into rubber seed oil methyl ester at different dosage involving 25 and 50 ppm. 5.2% increase and 10.56% decrease were specified using B100T50. On the other hand, NOx raised by 21% whereas smoke, HC and CO reduced by 44%, 28% and 44% according to pure biodiesel at full load. Murugesan et al. [34] revealed that 8% and 1.6% improvement on BTE was enhanced with nanoparticle addition in diesel-waste cooking oil biodiesel mixture. Furthermore, it was found that smoke, UHC and CO decreased by 16%, 9% and 5% respectively. Jain et al. [1] researched the effects of titanium dioxide in diesel-Eichhornia crassipes biodiesel mixture on performance and emissions. They implied that BTE increased by 1.01% for 150 ppm nanoparticle addition according diesel at full load. In Ref. [35], aluminum oxide nanoparticle with QPAN80 blended with B20 showed improvement on combustion characteristics. B20Al2O350DSP100 (80% diesel+20% biodiesel+50 ppm aluminum oxide nanoparticle+100 ppm dispersant) provided maximum enhancement by 21.5%, 17.87%, and 15.16 % on mean gas temperature, net heat release rate and in-cylinder pressure respectively.

One of the promising nanomaterials for engines is CQDs. Quantum dots have small dimensions and are liquid [36]. Quantum dots can improve the thermal conductivity of the mixed liquid [37]. When these properties of quantum dots are evaluated, it is seen that they do not have a harmful effect on the engine parts. Moreover, the addition of nanoparticles such as CQD improves the lubricating properties of oils. Nanoparticles easily adhere to friction surfaces, forming a protective film that minimizes direct contact between surfaces. This reduces wear, corrosion, and oxidation on fuel contact surfaces [38–40]. In addition, CQDs can be cost-effectively integrated into fuel production processes due to their key advantages such as low toxicity, environmental friendliness, low production cost, and simple synthesis methods. Some of these synthesis methods include arc discharge, electrochemical approach, laser ablation, acid oxidation, hydrothermal, pyrolysis, solvothermal, microwave, and ultrasonic [41]. CQDs can be synthesized from biocompatible, economical, and renewable materials such as ethanol, citric acid, glucosamine, ascorbic acid, watermelon rind, fruit peels, orange juice, gelatin, sugar cane, and eggs [42,43]. These facts indicate that CQDs can be used as cost-effective additives or functional materials in fuel production processes without significant economic barriers [44–47]. In addition, cost and ease of production are important factors. Gavaskar et al. carried out environmentally friendly CQD production in a laboratory environment using a green synthesis method [48].

In this research, integrated assessment using diesel-microwave corn biodiesel (D80MCB20) mixture and carbon quantum dot nanoparticle was performed to observe the influences on combustion specifications such as cylinder pressure, heat release, etc., SFC, CO, CO2, HC, NOx and soot emissions at different engine loads in a diesel engine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave assisted biodiesel production by transesterification reaction
ce:section.ce:para: Biodiesel is produced from fats and oils by chemical or biochemical means [49,50]. Biodiesel is obtained using production methods such as transesterification, blending, microemulsion and pyrolysis [51–53]. Transesterification is the simplest and most economical way to produce environmentally friendly and safe fuel with physical properties similar to diesel fuel [54,55]. Conventional heating is widely used in the transesterification reaction. In the transesterification reaction using conventional heating, a large amount of energy is required as heat energy is transferred from the reactor surface to the mixture through convection, conduction and radiation [54]. Therefore, the transesterification reaction with conventional heating requires a longer reaction time. As an alternative to conventional heating, new technologies such as supercritical fluid, microwave irradiation and ultrasonic wave are used in biodiesel production [56]. Since energy is transmitted directly to the reactants in microwave-assisted transesterification reaction, heat transfer is more effective than conventional heating and the reaction is completed in a shorter time [54]. In addition, biodiesel with low energy consumption and high conversion efficiency is produced by microwave-assisted transesterification reactions [52,56,57].

Biodiesel production from corn oil was carried out using the microwave-assisted transesterification reaction. A modified household-type microwave (Samsung ME711K) with 100–800 W output power and 2450 Mhz operating frequency was used in the experiments. A reflux condenser was added to the modified household-type microwave, which allows the condensation of methanol evaporated during the reaction. The reaction was carried out in a glass reactor (250 ml flat-bottom glass flask). A magnetic stirrer and temperature sensor are placed at the bottom of the modified household-type microwave. This system is shown in Fig. 1 .

In the microwave-assisted tranesterification reaction, NaOH and methyl alcohol were used as catalyst and alcohol, respectively. The reaction was carried out using corn oil, 0.5 wt% NaOH and 25 wt% methanol. The catalyst and methyl alcohol mixture stirred on a hotplate with a magnetic stirrer under a condenser. Catalyst and methyl alcohol solution were added to preheated corn oil at 50 °C in a glass reactor in the microwave. The reaction was carried out at 100 W microwave output power, 500 rpm stirring speed, 60 °C temperature and 10 min reaction time. At the end of the reaction, the mixture was transferred to the separating funnel. After the glycerin layer was separated, the biodiesel was washed with pure water at 90 °C. At the end of this process, a layer of soap, water and biodiesel was formed. The biodiesel layer was separated and the washing process was repeated five times. The water and alcohol remaining in the biodiesel were evaporated by heating. Test fuel properties are shown in Table 1 . Test fuel abbreviations are shown in Table 2 .

The CQD used in this study was purchased from sigma-aldrich. CQD has properties such as >5% quantum efficiency, dispersion form, ≥0.2% concentration in H2O, and 450–550 nm fluorescence. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are typically spherical nanoparticles with structures ranging from amorphous cores to nanocrystals consisting predominantly of graphite (sp2 carbon), graphene, oxidized graphene or diamond-like hybrid carbon (sp³). These quantum dots usually have 1–20% weight fraction of oxygen-containing functional groups such as carboxyl (COOH) and hydroxyl (OH). Carbon quantum dots’ chemical structure is given in Fig. 2 [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Engine performance, combustion and emissions characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: The present work focuses on the influences of quantum dot nanoparticle addition into diesel-microwave corn biodiesel mixture on performance, combustion and emissions. For this reason a single cylinder DI diesel engine was mounted with regenerative dynamometer in the experiments. Carbon quantum dot nanoparticle was mixed with diesel-microwave corn biodiesel mixture (D80MCB20) in the amounts of 60, 120 and 180 ppm. The maximum torque of the engine is 20.60 Nm. Test torque values are defined according to 25%, 50%, 75% and 100% motor load. Test engine was run at 2200 rpm and 4.12, 9.61, 15.10, and 20.60 Nm with diesel, diesel-microwave corn biodiesel mixture (D80MCB20) and D80MCB20 CQD 60 ppm, D80MCB20 CQD 120 ppm, D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm test fuels. To eliminate the measurement differences, test engine was heated up and values were recorded. Experimental setup is given in Fig. 3 . Tests were performed using an Antor AD510 BS DI diesel engine. The properties of the test engine are also given in Table 3 .
Bosch BEA 350 gas analyzer determined the HC, CO, CO2, soot and NOx level. Table 4 presents the specifications of the exhaust gas analyzer.

In-cylinder pressure was specified using AVL QC34C in-cylinder pressure sensor. Kistler 5018 A amplified the pressure data. Digital data from analog data was obtained using National Instrument 6351 data acqusition card. Besides, TFA ST-490 infrared thermometer was used to detect engine block temperature. Heat release rate was computed dependent on the first law of thermodynamic as mentioned in Eq (1) [59–62].

The influences of carbon quantum dot into D80MCB20 on heat release and cylinder pressure are given in Fig. 4 . It was found that higher in-cylinder pressure was measured with pure diesel according to fuel mixtures. Maximum in-cylinder pressure raised with the increase of engine load. Combustion was retarded using D80MCB20 according to diesel for all loads. High density and viscosity of biodiesel deteriorated the pulverization and combustion processes. Test outcomes showed that carbon quantum dot nanoparticle addition had remarkable influence on cylinder pressure when comparing D80MCB20. The addition of nanoparticle clearly caused to raise in-cylinder pressure compared to D80MCB20. It is clear to report that the disadvantages of biodiesel such as higher viscosity and density was compensated with the nanoparticle addition into D80MCB20. In this regard, the presence of carbon quantum dot nanoparticle promoted the oxidation reactions resulting in improved combustion. In-cylinder pressure raised with the rise of nanoparticle additives in the fuel blends. It can be said that nanoparticle improved the physicochemical specifications of biodiesel and enhanced the combustion phasing.

The cylinder pressure values and change rates were given in Table 5 . In the tests carried out under different load conditions, improvements of 2.77–11.48%, 8.01–9.63% and 5.96–9.39% were observed with the addition of 60, 120 and 180 ppm CQD to D80MCB20 fuel, respectively.

ID is highly critical variable for compression ignition engines. Combustion development is dependent on ID during combustion. Fig. 5 gives the changes of ID. As expected, it was found that maximum ID was determined using D80MCB20 owing to higher viscosity and density of biodiesel. Atomization and homogeneity of the charge composition with biodesel are worsened and evoparation and forming the first flame kernel processes are prolonged. The most important finding related to ID is to become shorter with the addition of nanoparticle into D80MCB20. D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm presented a slight increase in ID compared to D80MCB20 CQD 120 ppm. It can be reported that nanoparticle shorten the evaporation stage of the charge mixture during combustion and ID period gets shorter. Chemical reaction rate is enhanced with the help of radiation and thermal characteristics of nanoparticle. So, the development of self-ignition behaviour is promoted.

Specific fuel consumption (SFC) is related to consumed fuel for producing unit power per time. It reflects the fuel economy. Fig. 6 represents the variations of SFC depending on engine load. It was found that the lowest SFC was achieved using diesel. The addition of biodiesel caused to raise SFC due to lower heating value and higher density of corn biodiesel according to diesel. Higher SFC was obtained using test fuels with nanoparticle additives. Nevertheless, a decreasing trend was realized with the carbon quantum dot nanoparticle additive into D80MCB20. SFC decreased with the rise of nanoparticle fraction in D80MCB20. SFC changed by 1.64%, −0.65% and −1.31% with D80MCB20 CQD 60 ppm, D80MCB20 CQD 120 ppm and D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm compared to D80MCB20 at 15.10 Nm respectively. It can be emphasized that nanoparticles act like energy carrier and oxidation reactions are accelerated. It provides more reactivation resulting in better combustion. Hence, more power from the fuel blend can be generated with the addition of nanoparticle.

The changes of CA50 and thermal efficiency are demonstrated in Fig. 7 . CA50 is a valuable combustion parameter where half of the charge mixture is carried out to ignite versus crank angle. As shown in Fig. 7, CA50 becomes longer with the rise of engine load. The minimum CA50 was obtained using D80MCB20, while the longest CA50 was determined for diesel for each engine load. Carbon quantum dot nanoparticle induced to decrease CA50 owing to catalyst and energy carrier characteristics. Better thermal conductivity and reactive nature of nanoparticle expedited the chemical reactions and CA50 took shorter time. Ignition development is improved with nanoparticle additives due to the heat and reactivity of nanoparticle. Higher thermal efficiency can be observed in case CA50 is obtained slightly ATDC.

Fig. 8 reflects the changes of combustion duration (CD) using test fuels. CD takes longer time to complete the combustion at higher engine loads owing to the more charged mixture. The longest CD was specified with diesel while the shortest CD for D80MCB20 at 20.60 Nm. It was explicitly seen that the addition of nanoparticle caused to decline in CD period due to improved catalytic activation, high diffusion and developed heat transfer. CD was shortened with the rise of amount of nanoparticle in the fuel mixture. In addition, nanoparticles improve the thermophysical specifications of the fuel.

Cyclic variations of imep are given in Fig. 9 . Cyclic variations originate from the differences on thermodynamic state and charge composition for each cycle. Cyclic variations should not exceed 10% for smooth and stable running of the engine [59]. As given in Fig. 9, the lowest COVimep was determined with diesel according to fuel mixtures. The rise of nanoparticle additives in the fuel mixture caused to increase cyclic variations. This situation is attributed to the high reactivity of nanoparticle. Higher reactivation and improved combustion reactions increase the differences during combustion cycle to cycle. It was seen that the limit value of 10% for cyclic variation was surpassed with D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm for 15.10 Nm, 9.61 Nm, 4.12 Nm and 4.12 Nm with D80MCB20 CQD 120 ppm for 4.12 Nm. Fig. 10 shows the changes of maximum pressure rise rate (MPRR). It was found that MPRR increased with the addition of nanoparticle into D80MCB20. Higher reactivation energy of nanoparticle causes to increase reaction rate. In addition, nanoparticles provided a higher chemical reaction due to higher surface area. It results rapid combustion and higher pressure rise rate. It was concluded from Fig. 10 that the highest MPRR was determined using D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm for each engine load. The lowest MPRR was specified using neat diesel.

The changes of ringing intensity (RI) are exhibited in Fig. 11 . The increase of engine load caused to raise RI for test fuels. The lowest RI was specified using diesel whereas the highest RI was obtained with D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm at 20.60 Nm. It was noticed that nanoparticle promoted the combustion and RI increased. The rise of nanoparticle in the fuel blend caused to raise RI. It can be mentioned that nanoparticle improves the ignition characteristics. Nanoparticle also acted like oxidation catalyst and accelerated the oxidation reaction between fuel and oxygen molecules. It was also considered that the higher thermal conductivity of nanoparticle helped to obtain peak pressure compared to diesel and D80MCB20. It was also mentioned that nanoparticle additive caused to obtain microexplosions and increase the heat relase rate and in-cylinder pressure during combustion. These phenomena promoted the combustion development and performance with the usage of nanoparticle. The existence of higher reactive surface area with nanoparticle promoted the oxidation reactions resulting in improved combustion [63–70].

The changes of CO and CO2 depending on engine load are seen in Fig. 12 . CO is produced due to insufficient temperature and oxygen during combustion. It was found that CO increased with the rise of engine load. Besides, it was seen that DD80MCB20 presented higher CO compared to other test fuels. Though biodiesel involves a higher amount of oxygen, higher CO is emitted due to poor atomization and lower calorific value of biodiesel according to diesel. The gas temperature remained partially low with biodiesel addition. A significant finding was found that nanoparticle addition caused to decrease CO emission. CO declined by 0.76%, 18.02% and 20.30% using D80MCB20 CQD 60 ppm, D80MCB20 CQD 120 ppm and D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm according to D80MCB20 at 20.60 Nm respectively. Higher reactivation energy improved the chemical reactions with higher gas temperatures in the combustion chamber. Hence, CO was reduced as seen in Fig. 12-a. There was an inverse relationship between CO and CO2. CO2 increased with the fuel mixtures compared that diesel. Furthermore, CO2 was produced more with the addition of nanoparticle into D80MCB20. The highest CO2 was measured using D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm. CO2 formation was accelerated using nanoparticle due to higher reactivation energy of nanoparticle. CO2 raised by 7.39%, 12.29% and 18.59% using D80MCB20 CQD 60 ppm, D80MCB20 CQD 120 ppm and D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm according to D80MCB20 at 20.60 Nm respectively. Fig. 13 depicts the changes of HC with test fuels. The highest HC was measured with D80MCB20 according to other test fuels. Poor atomization characteristics of biodiesel prevented to obtain homogeneous charge and oxidation reactions weakened in piston and ring gaps, valve edges and cavities. Thus, HC was formed. It was reported that HC reduced with the usage of nanoparticle. Nanoparticle compensated the poor specifications of biodiesel and enhanced the oxidation reactions with higher thermal conductivity. It also acted as an energy carrier during combustion [70–72]. HC reduced by 8.33–19.48%, 12.5–28.57% and 16.67–31.16% with D80MCB20 CQD 60 ppm, D80MCB20 CQD 120 ppm and D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm according to D80MCB20 under the different loads respectively.

Fig. 14 depicts the changes of NOx and soot emissions obtained from test fuels. A clear increase was observed on NOx emissions with fuel mixtures according to diesel as seen in Fig. 14-a. The addition of biodiesel raised NOx. Ince et al. observed similar NOx results after the use of biodiesel [73]. In addition, ID perios was prolonged owing to bad atomization and chemical decomposition of biodiesel. Higher gas temperature is obtained during combustion because the whole mixture intends to ignite suddenly. Furthermore, the increase of nanoparticle addition in D80MCB20 caused to raise NOx. Activation energy and the improvement of ignition characteristics of fuel mixture promoted the oxidation reactions. Therefore, as a result of combustion, the in-cylinder gas temperature was higher and NOx was formed. NOx raised by 6.38–48.29%, 20.57–55.44% and 49.29–56.26% using D80MCB20 CQD 60 ppm, D80MCB20 CQD 120 ppm and D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm compared to D80MCB20 under the different loads. CQDs with different sizes can be tried to reduce the increase in NOx emissions. It is possible to synthesize CQDs with different sizes by changing the synthesis parameters [74,75]. Soot was also examined with test fuels and compared to diesel as shown in Fig. 14-b. Soot is generated by fuel injection system failures, fuel characteristics or rich mixtures [36,40–42]. Soot emissions were reduced too much with nanoparticle addition. Chemical reactions were improved and forming soot reduced with the usage of nanoparticle. Soot emission decreased by 3.70–33.33%, 29.62–49,67% and 36.66–66.67% with D80MCB20 CQD 60 ppm, D80MCB20 CQD 120 ppm and D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm, according to D80MCB20 under the different load.

The effects of CQD and metal oxide added nanofuels on engine performance, combustion and emissions are compared in Table 6 . In the engine test results with the fuel mixture investigated in this study, an increase in CP, SFC, CA50, CD, COVimep, MPRR, RI, CO2 and NOx values and a decrease in CO, HC and soot emissions were observed. Venu et al. [76] added TiO2 nanoadditive to CEME fuel. They observed an increase in engine CP, BTE, BSFC, CO2 and NOx values and a decrease in HC and Co values with TiO2 nanoadditive. Sathish et al. [77] investigated the effect on the engine by creating WCO biodiesel + Diesel + CNT fuel mixture. In their study, they observed that CNT additives caused an increase in BSFC, BTE, HC values and an improvement in CO, NOx emissions. Mohan et al. [78] added CeO2 metal oxide additive to orange peel biodiesel fuel. This additive caused an increase in engine BSFC and BTE values while improving CO and NOx emissions. Bitire et al. [79] added CuO to Parsely biodiesel + Diesel blend fuel and achieved improvement in CO, NOx and HC emissions. However, they observed an increase in BSFC, BTE and CO2 emissions.

The scope of this exhaustive research is to investigate the influences of carbon quantum dot nanoparticle additive into diesel-microwave corn biodiesel mixture (D80MCB20) on combustion, performance and emissions in a diesel engine. Test findings presented that the rise of nanoparticle addition caused to increase in-cylinder pressure compared to D80MCB20. ID period was shortened in case nanoparticle was added into diesel-biodiesel fuel blend owing to higher reactivation energy. SFC decreased by 0.65% and 1.31% using D80MCB20 CQD 120 ppm and D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm according to D80MCB20 at 15.10 Nm respectively. Higher CD period was determined using test fuels with nanoparticle additive. But, the increase of nanoparticle amount caused to decrease CD. Besides, MPRR and RI increased with nanoparticle addition owing to higher reactivation energy of carbon quantum dot nanoparticle. Test results also exhibited that CO reduced by 0.76%, 18.02% and 20.30% using D80MCB20 CQD 60 ppm, D80MCB20 CQD 120 ppm and D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm according to D80MCB20 at 20.60 Nm respectively. HC was also decreased by 19.48%, 28.57% and 31.16% with D80MCB20 CQD 60 ppm, D80MCB20 CQD 120 ppm and D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm according to D80MCB20 at 20.60 Nm respectively while NOx raised by 48.29%, 55.44% and 56.26% using D80MCB20 CQD 60 ppm, D80MCB20 CQD 120 ppm and D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm according to D80MCB20 at 20.60 Nm. Soot emission significantly declined by about 3.70%, 29.62% and 36.66% with D80MCB20 CQD 60 ppm, D80MCB20 CQD 120 ppm and D80MCB20 CQD 180 ppm compared to D80MCB20 at 20.60 Nm. It was found that, carbon quantum dot nanoparticle compensated the poor properties of diesel-microwave corn biodiesel mixture and improved the chemical oxidation reactions in a diesel engine. As a result, carbon quantum dot nanoparticle has a strong potential to enhance performance and reduce CO, HC and soot emissions in a diesel engine. To determine the optimal concentration of carbon quantum dot (CQD) additives, it is essential to evaluate performance, fuel consumption, and emission parameters. The results indicate that fuels containing 120 and 180 ppm CQD additives exhibited better performance compared to those with 60 ppm additives. Notably, the lowest specific fuel consumption (SFC) was achieved with the fuel containing 180 ppm CQD additives. Additionally, this formulation yielded the lowest emissions of carbon monoxide (CO), hydrocarbons (HC), and soot. Therefore, it can be concluded that the most effective fuel mixture for this study is D80MCB20 with 180 ppm CQD additives. In this study, it was concluded that the CQDs examined had a positive effect on diesel engines. The preparation processes of CQD-added fuels are simple. It has been mentioned in many studies in the literature that the use of CQDs improves lubricity. This improvement will contribute to reducing corrosion and wear on the engine parts in contact with the fuel. Currently, there is no mass production of CQDs. Despite the easy production of CQDs and the abundance of raw materials, their production costs are therefore high. Before applying them to vehicles, it is essential to carry out a mass production plan and feasibility study of CQDs. In the first stage, it is recommended that the system and substructure be established, and a situation assessment be carried out for companies with flo vehicles. To reduce the increase in NOx emissions, it is aimed to examine the fuel injection advance in the next studies. During the study process, it was realized that CQD size and modifications may influence the chemical structure. A study on CQD with different sizes and modified is planned for the next studies.
